reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Lead paragraph concisely explains both what the command does and the key behavioral difference from resolving (ignored issues never reopen), giving the reader exactly the context they need to decide whether to use it.
Correct use of <CliCommandsApiReference> with all four flags sourced accurately from commands.ts (issueId required, agent/logLevel/outputFormat optional) — no hand-authored flags table.
'When to Ignore vs Resolve' section provides genuine decision support with concrete examples (client-side 4xx errors, third-party SDK warnings) rather than circular reasoning.
Related commands section links the three closest siblings (list, resolve, reopen) which covers the full issues workflow cleanly.
Each bash block contains exactly one command with no explanatory # comments inside — copy button works correctly on all three examples.
problems: Minor redundancy: the first example in the 'Examples' section (`stacktape issues:ignore --issueId iss_abc123def456`) is functionally identical to the 'Usage' section command — it adds nothing beyond swapping the placeholder for a concrete ID. Section instructions say 'Examples if there are non-obvious patterns'; this first example is not non-obvious.

The page does not mention the success output format. The source (issues-ignore/index.ts) shows the CLI prints 'Issue {issueId} ignored.' A one-line callout or prose note about expected output would help users confirm the command ran correctly, especially in scripted contexts.

The 'Flags Reference' heading label is slightly non-standard for this doc surface — the section instructions don't specify a heading name but 'Flags' or 'Options' would be more conventional and consistent with other CLI pages.
optionalImprovements: Remove or replace the first 'Examples' entry (plain `--issueId iss_abc123def456`) since it duplicates 'Usage'. Substitute a genuinely non-obvious pattern instead — for example, showing `--outputFormat jsonl` for CI pipelines that need to parse the response, or `--logLevel debug` for troubleshooting.

Add a one-sentence note after the examples (or inline in the success output) describing what the CLI prints on success: 'Issue iss_abc123def456 ignored.' This anchors user confidence in scripted workflows and helps with debugging when the command is called from CI.
Consider renaming 'Flags Reference' to 'Flags' or 'Options' for consistency with the short-label heading convention (1-5 words, noun form) used across CLI reference pages.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All four flags from the source (`issueId`, `agent`, `logLevel`, `outputFormat`) are correctly represented in the `<CliCommandsApiReference>` component and match the source `commands.ts` definition exactly.
The two bash examples are correctly split into separate fenced blocks with prose descriptions before each — no copy-button trap.
The 'When to Ignore vs Resolve' section is directly grounded in both command descriptions from `commands.ts` and adds genuine decision value without inventing behavior.
The 'permanently silences until you explicitly reopen them' framing is accurate — `issues:reopen` exists as a sibling command and is linked correctly.
Related commands section links all three peer commands with accurate one-line descriptions drawn from source.
Opening paragraph is concise and self-contained; the distinction from `issues:resolve` is surfaced immediately rather than buried.
problems: Completeness gap: the page has no FAQ section. The style guide requires one on reference pages. For a narrow CLI command this would be 4-6 pairs, not 10, but even a short FAQ ('What happens to existing alerts for an ignored issue?', 'How do I find the issue ID?', 'Can I bulk-ignore issues?', 'How is ignore different from resolve?') would significantly improve AEO discoverability.

The H2 heading 'When to Ignore vs Resolve' is a question/comparison form rather than the preferred short noun-form label (e.g. 'Ignore vs Resolve'). Not a major violation but drifts from the '1-5 word noun-form labels' guidance for H2s.

The `Usage` section does not mention the optional flags at all — a reader scanning just that section would not know `--agent` or `--outputFormat` exist. At minimum, a note like 'see Flags Reference below for optional flags' would help.
optionalImprovements: Add a short FAQ section (4-6 pairs) covering: (1) How is ignore different from resolve? (2) How do I find the issue ID to pass to --issueId? (3) What happens to alerts already sent for an ignored issue? (4) Can I reopen an ignored issue later? (5) Does ignore affect all stages or just one? — these are the questions a developer will Google when deciding whether to use this command.
Shorten the H2 'When to Ignore vs Resolve' to a noun-form label such as 'Ignore vs Resolve' to match the recommended heading style.

Add a one-liner after the `Usage` block noting that optional flags (agent mode, output format, log level) are documented in the Flags Reference below — prevents the usage section from looking like the command only takes one argument.

Consider noting in the intro or examples that the `--issueId` value is obtained from the `issues:list` command output — the page links it but doesn't make the workflow explicit. A short sentence like 'Copy the ID from `stacktape issues:list` output and pass it here' makes the workflow concrete.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes ignoring from resolving.
Usage and examples show the required `--issueId` flag, and each shell block contains one copyable command.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="issues:ignore" ... />` component and avoids forbidden Stacktape config patterns.
Related commands are relevant and help the reader move between list, resolve, and reopen workflows.
problems: The word "permanently" is too strong without immediate qualification because the source also documents `issues:reopen` for reopening ignored issues.

The examples in "When to Ignore vs Resolve" mention expected client-side 4xx errors, third-party SDK warnings, and edge-case timeouts, but those scenarios are not grounded in the provided source files.
The draft says ignored issues "stay silent permanently until you explicitly reopen them," which is clearer than the opener; the page should use that narrower wording consistently.
The page could better surface the actual success behavior from `src/commands/issues-ignore/index.ts`: the command calls `ignoreIssue({ issueId })` and prints `Issue <id> ignored.`
mandatoryFixes: Replace unsupported broad scenario examples with source-grounded wording, or phrase them cautiously as examples of reviewed non-actionable runtime errors without implying Stacktape classifies those cases specially.
Change the opening "permanently silences" wording to "marks a runtime issue as ignored" and immediately state that ignored issues can be reopened with `issues:reopen`.
optionalImprovements: Add a short "Output" or sentence after usage noting that a successful command prints `Issue <issue-id> ignored.`, grounded in the command source.
Move or rename "When to Ignore vs Resolve" before examples if the intended flow is decision first, command examples second.
Mention the short alias `--iid` in prose near the usage example, since the generated reference exposes it.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly consistent with the provided CLI command sources. The main factual risk is wording that overstates how permanent ignoring is, because the same provided command set includes `issues:reopen` for ignored issues.

issues.statement: "The `issues:ignore` command permanently silences a runtime issue..." and "Ignored issues stay silent permanently until you explicitly reopen them."
issues.reason: The source supports that ignored issues do not reopen on new occurrences and do not generate alerts, but it also documents `issues:reopen` for ignored issues. Calling the state "permanent" is stronger than the source wording and can mislead readers into thinking the action is irreversible.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace "permanently silences" with "marks a runtime issue as ignored" or "keeps a reviewed issue silent until you reopen it." Keep the explicit reopen caveat near the first paragraph.

evidence.quote: Marks an issue as ignored. Ignored issues will not reopen on new occurrences and won't generate alerts.
Reopens a resolved or ignored issue, setting its status back to OPEN.

positiveFindings: The usage example includes the required `--issueId` flag, matching `requiredArgs: ['issueId']`.
The flags table uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="issues:ignore" ... />`, which is the expected component for a CLI reference page.
The documented allowed flags match the command definition: `logLevel`, `agent`, `outputFormat`, and `issueId`.
Cross-links point to routes present in the provided navigation index.
The draft does not include forbidden Stacktape configuration examples, YAML config blocks, or plain-object `StacktapeConfig` usage.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly consistent with the provided CLI command definitions and command implementation. I found one support gap around the hand-specified flag metadata: the command and utility sources confirm the allowed argument names and required `issueId`, but the provided source set does not include the option definitions that establish the aliases and long descriptions shown in `sortedArgs`.

issues.statement: The `<CliCommandsApiReference>` `sortedArgs` metadata lists specific aliases and descriptions, including `issueId` alias `iid`, `agent` alias `ag`, `logLevel` alias `ll`, and `outputFormat` alias `ofmt`.
issues.reason: The provided command definition supports the argument names and required `issueId`, but aliases and detailed option descriptions are derived from `argAliases` and option schemas in `src/config/cli/options`, which is referenced but not included in the provided source files. This makes the alias values and long descriptions unverifiable from the supplied source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Either verify these values against `src/config/cli/options.ts`, or omit hand-authored `sortedArgs` details if the docs renderer can generate them directly from the CLI metadata.

positiveFindings: The usage example correctly includes the only required command-specific flag: `--issueId`.
The draft correctly states that ignored issues do not reopen on new occurrences and do not generate alerts.
The distinction between `issues:ignore`, `issues:resolve`, and `issues:reopen` matches the provided command descriptions.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the documentation navigation index.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="issues:ignore" ... />` component and documents exactly one CLI command.

verifierResults.summary: The page is mostly complete for the `issues:ignore` CLI surface: it documents the single required `issueId` argument, includes the generated CLI reference component, and provides focused examples. I found one wording issue that could mislead users about reversibility.

issues.statement: “The `issues:ignore` command permanently silences a runtime issue...”
issues.reason: The command does mark an issue as ignored, and ignored issues do not reopen on new occurrences or generate alerts. However, the source also defines `issues:reopen` as reopening a resolved or ignored issue, so “permanently” overstates the behavior unless qualified.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace “permanently silences” with “silences” or “keeps silent until you reopen it.” For example: “The `issues:ignore` command silences a runtime issue until you explicitly reopen it.”

evidence.quote:
'issues:ignore': {
    description: `Marks an issue as ignored. Ignored issues will not reopen on new occurrences and won't generate alerts.`

'issues:reopen': {
    description: `Reopens a resolved or ignored issue, setting its status back to OPEN.`

positiveFindings: The page documents exactly one command, `issues:ignore`, matching the CLI-page instruction.
The usage and examples include the only required flag, `--issueId`, which is grounded in `requiredArgs: ['issueId']`.
The page uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="issues:ignore" ... />` instead of hand-authoring a flags table.
The flags reference includes all documented arguments for the command: `issueId`, `agent`, `logLevel`, and `outputFormat`.
